We have received the following letter:—‘ Piano-
forte Organ, and Harmonium Warerooms, 4 and
5 Westmoreland Strect, Dublin, 23rd February,
1912. Dear Sir,—We have noticed in the cur-
rent issue of the Lmaper (February 24th) a review
of some recently published hymns, which is calculated
to leave your readers under erroncous impressions as
to previously published works of the same kind. We
shall, therefore, feel much obliged if you will find room
for a statement of the following facts by way of recti-
fication:—(1) We are the publishers of a hymn to Sb.
Patrick, the melody an ancient Irish one, the arrange-
ment and English words by the Rev. Geo. O'Neill,
S.J., Trish words by Lady O’Donoghue. This hymn
has already had a very wide circulation, is well known
in the principal Dublin churches, and had the honour of
boing selected as the hymn sung ab the consecration of
Armagh Cathedral in 1904. (2) We are publishers of
a hymn to St. Brigid, the melody an ancient Irish one,
arrangement and English words by Rev. Geo. O’Neill,
S.J., Irish words by Dr. Conor Maguire. In the
opinion of many it deserves a popularity greater even
than that of its companion.: Both hymns have been
honoured with commendation in the columns of the
Leaper.  Thanking you in anticipation—We remain,
dear sir, yours very faithfully—Crayer Woop & Co."

In May, 1908, a competitive examination was held
in Cork for clerkships in the Cork, Bandon and South
Coast Railway. The two most successful candidates
were Catholics and were appointed to the Traffic De-
partment almost immediately, the third man, who
happened to be a non-Catholic, received an appointment
about eight months afterwards, and within twelve
months afterwards received a favoured position in the
traffic manager’s office. Perhaps being a non-Catholic
he had far greater moral and intellectual fibre than
those who beat him in the competitive examination!
In the earlier part of 1911 another examination was
held. The most successful candidates again were
Catholics and received similar appointments: the can-
didate who figured fourth on the results list is a non-
Catholic and was recently appointed to a position in
the traffic manager’s office. In the Cork terminus and
head offices of this railway we hear that all the best
paid positions, without exception, are held by non-
We would like some more information
concerning the staffing of this Cork, Bandon and South
Coast Railway.

The recent appeal on behalf of the Sacred Heart
Home, Drumcondra, has resulted in a very good re-
sponse, as will be seen by the acknowledgments in
another column. In these days of barefaced prosely-
tism this charity has a special claim on the Catholic
public. There are over 200 children at present in the
Home. Subsecriptions may be sent divect to the Pre-
sident of the Home, Miss O’Brien, 94 Lower Baggot
Street, Dublin.

A stick of Arbutus shaving soap and some squares
of Arbutus toilet soap are some of the latest works of
art that we have been reviewing. Arbutus soaps are
high class products of one of our most enterprising
Trish soap manufacturers, Messrs. Dixon and Co., of
Dublin. The Arbutus shaving sticks are made up in
neat cylinder cases and are retailed at 1s. per stick.
A learned and solemn judge recently expressed some per-
sonal opinions from the Bench on razors. So we may
be excused if we venture to say that a good soap is
only second to a good razor. And if Arbutus shaving
soap is not good enough for any judge in the land, all
we can say is, he is a poor judge of soap.  Arbutus
toilet tablets are sold at 4d. each retail, and are a credit
to the Irish soap industry.

WOMEN AND THE INSURANCE ACT.

THE question of the Status of Women looms so

large on the horizon of Ireland at present, that
one needs no excuse for dealing with any phase of the
subject, whether new or old. = Therefore, I, greatly
daring, infend to examine briefly here the effect the
new Insurance Act will have on their position.

For the purposes of the Act, women may be divided
into two classes—those who earn their living, or at
least supplement it by their earnings, and those who
live at home in sheltered ease. With the latter, the
Act is, of course, no more concerned than it is with
their brothers in idleness. The former we must, how-
ever, unlike the male ‘class corresponding to it, divide
into married women and spinsters, the income limit
for both being the same as for men. The case of the
unmarried worker is the less complicated, so it may be
taken first. It will also form a useful standard of com-
parison when we come to deal with the married women.

As in the case of the male worker, the employer's
contribution will be 2id. a week, and the State’s 2d.,
but the employee herself will, in normal cases, have
to pay only 2d. a week, as against a male employee’s
3d. The amount will, as in the man’s case, be deducted
by her employer from her wages on the day that she iy
paid, whether such payment is made weekly or other
wise, and special tax stamps for the combined contri:
butions of herself and her employer will have to be
affixed by the latter to a book provided for the pur-
pose, and the Act has special safeguards against the
employer’s stopping his contribution out of the worker’s
wages, or vice versa. As to arrears, I will merely re-
capitulate what I have said in a former article—ar

SIMPLIFIED SPELLING (ENGLISH).

\/\'T E hear a good deal nowadays about Simplified

Spelling in connection with the Trish Language.
Sufficient attention has not, however, been paid to the
movement which has for some time past been on foot in
this country to simplify spelling in English.  There
is no definite authority behind this movement. It
has no committee to guide its progress or regulate its
procedure. “Many people no doubt are quite unaware
of its existence. The careful student of the times,
however, must have detected it. So far it is confined
most solely to the Press, and very largely to the half-
penny Press.  This spelling reform follows the line
advocated by Hamlet in his celebrated advice to the
players. It is a move in the direction of reforming
things altogether.” So far from spelling a word at all—
even in an abbreviated form—the tendency to which we
allude is to give merely the initial letter of the tord.
Among Irvish Press writers of late this habit has become
extraordinarily prevalent. This witty style—for brevity
we are assured is the soul of wit—is now quite the
mode, and constitutes yet a further Irish paradox illu-
strating at one and the same time that we are witty, as
alleged, and yet that we are not prolix, as'is also as-
serted.  To convey clearly the extent to which this
new style has crept in, T have adopted it in telling the
following nonsensical tale: —

-'D_em§ Malome was revolving the problem of what to

for some time as a P.G. He was living al this period

] RIAD with pleasure the article of Sappan. and 1
- admire his enthusiasm for the games he plays.

T set out to challenge the claim of the G.A.A. man
to the title of Gael.

I deny the Anglicised hurler the right to call an Trish
speaker ‘‘ West Briton '’ merely because he play;
Rugby. I say again that “ Gall,”” Mr. Walsh, and
Sappan  do not understand the full significance of the
terms West Briton, Anglicisation and Irish Ireland.
Once more I refer them to * The Philosophy of Irish
Treland ** for the information they stand in need of.

, *“ the Gael that cannot give up Rugby
v weakling. , the hurler that cannot afford
an hour or two in the week for the study of the National

tongue and the history of his native land is a poor -

specimen of an Irishman. There is no necessity to

ell Patrick’s Place to teach the language of the Gael

it is already doing so. It would be more useful if

A. told its members to insist on ir brothers

and sisters getting a knowledge of elic in the

of the leading G.A.A. pavish in Cork City, where a
word of Gaelic has not yet been taught and where
there is no intention of teaching it.

Though your contributor signs himself Saprdan
he has already learned one trick of newspaper contro-
versy, that is the knack of quoting out of context.
He writes thus:—'* He (Comzpom na Fémne ) knows
‘one Rugby player in Cork with an acquired know-
ledge of Irish.’ ”’ He omitted to add thai when I re-
ferred to the Rugby player with an acquired knowledge
of Trish I claimed for him that he was willing and
capable of teaching the “‘ Gaels '’ of the Cork County
Board the language of the Gael provided they joined
some branch of the Gaelic League.

The amount of past and present players of Irish
games T know is out of all proportion to the Rugby men
T know, but the percentage of the latter who are inte-
rested in the language revival is far higher than that of
the former.
_ The Welsh people, though proud of their own lan-
gunage, play Rugby far more than the Trish people do.

men in Cork do not care a pin whether Gaglic lives or
dies.  Sappan puts all the blame for their ignorance
of it on the schools, as if a language could be preserved
by being used only in the schools.

THE GAS INDUSTRY IN IRELAND.

DEsr Sm,—I have read in the last issue of your
paper that the Gag Committee of Clonmel has exploded
and that a new committee has been appointed.

_Let us hope that the newly-clected committee
give a better account of themselves.

I should strongly advise the present committee to
get in toueh with an Trish gas engineer, such as the
engineer and manager of the Wexford Gas Co., whose
advice has been sought on gas matters in Ireland, and
no doubt he will be able to supply Clonmel with a
capable gas manager from the eight Ivish applicants.

The present committee should insist on an Irishman
getting a chance of displaying his worth in the manage-
ment of gas affairs, and they may feel confident of the
support of the majority of the Corporvation and every
thinking man in Clonmel.

I have also read in the same issue a letter from a
pondent, ‘‘ Interested.”” No doubt he takes a
broad view of the whole question. Evidently he

ave had in his mind a different firm of gas engi-
